papergraph.title: Multiobjective energy management of multi-source offshore parks assisted with hybrid battery and hydrogen/fuel-cell energy storage systems
papergraph.citation: E. Kazemi-Robati et al. (2025) Multiobjective energy management of multi-source offshore parks assisted with hybrid battery and hydrogen/fuel-cell energy storage systems doi:10.1016/j.apenergy.2024.124529
papergraph.abstract: With the recent advancements in the development of hybrid offshore parks and the expected large-scale implementation of them in the near future, it becomes paramount to investigate proper energy management strategies to improve the integrability of these parks into the power systems. This paper addresses a multi-objective energy management approach using a hybrid energy storage system comprising batteries and hydrogen/fuel-cell systems applied to multi-source wind-wave and wind-solar offshore parks to maximize the delivered energy while minimizing the variations of the power output. To find the solution of the optimization problem defined for energy management, a strategy is proposed based on the examination of a set of weighting factors to form the Pareto front while the problem associated with each of them is assessed in a mixed-integer linear programming framework. Subsequently, fuzzy decision making is applied to select the final solution among the ones existing in the Pareto front. The studies are implemented in different locations considering scenarios for electrical system limitation and the place of the storage units. According to the results, applying the proposed multiobjective framework successfully addresses the enhancement of energy delivery and the decrease in power output fluctuations in the hybrid offshore parks across all scenarios of electrical system limitation and combinational storage locations. Based on the results, in addition to the increase in delivered energy, a decrease in power variations by around 40 % up to over 80 % is observed in the studied cases.

papergraph.title: The Russia-Ukraine Conflict: Its Implications for the Global Food Supply Chains
papergraph.citation: Sandeep Jagtap et al. (2022) The Russia-Ukraine Conflict: Its Implications for the Global Food Supply Chains doi:10.3390/foods11142098
papergraph.abstract: Food is one of the most traded goods, and the conflict in Ukraine, one of the European breadbaskets, has triggered a significant additional disruption in the global food supply chains after the COVID-19 impact. The disruption to food output, supply chains, availability, and affordability could have a long-standing impact. As a result, the availability and supply of a wide range of food raw materials and finished food products are under threat, and global markets have seen recent increases in food prices. Furthermore, the Russian-Ukrainian conflict has adversely affected food supply chains, with significant effects on production, sourcing, manufacturing, processing, logistics, and significant shifts in demand between nations reliant on imports from Ukraine. This paper aims to analyze the impacts of the conflict between Russia and Ukraine on the effectiveness and responsiveness of the global food supply chains. A PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) approach, including grey literature, was deployed to investigate six key areas of the food supply chains that would be impacted most due to the ongoing war. Findings include solutions and strategies to mitigate supply chain impacts such as alternative food raw materials, suppliers and supply chain partners supported by technological innovations to ensure food safety and quality in warlike situations.

papergraph.title: Assessing impacts of responsible sourcing initiatives for cobalt: Insights from a case study
papergraph.citation: L. Mancini et al. (2021) Assessing impacts of responsible sourcing initiatives for cobalt: Insights from a case study doi:10.1016/J.RESOURPOL.2021.102015
papergraph.abstract: Abstract Used in e-mobility and electronics, batteries are key technologies in the decarbonisation of the economy and in accomplishing the aims of the European Green Deal. Cobalt is one of the materials used in lithium-ion batteries, and its extraction is a concern in terms of human rights abuses, child labour, and tragic working conditions particularly in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) which supplies around 60% of the world's supply, with a relevant share of Artisanal and Small-scale Mining (ASM). Responding to these risks, some programs for the responsible sourcing of cobalt have been launched in recent years. Several policy initiatives at EU level aim to promote ethical sourcing of battery raw materials. In describing the results of research into the responsible and sustainable sourcing of cobalt for batteries, this article focuses on the artisanal mining sector in the DRC. Based on primary and secondary data collection and assessment of improvement against the requirements of responsible sourcing (RS) frameworks and sustainability assessment guidance (including Social Life Cycle Assessment, S-LCA), this study compares the situation of two pilot projects to the general situation at cobalt ASM sites in the Katangese Copperbelt. Specifically, the study examines the implementation of the Mutoshi Cobalt Pilot (MCP) and Better Mining at the Kasulo site, both in Lualaba province. The case study then provides the basis for discussing the lessons learned for the assessment and monitoring of responsible sourcing programs and of due diligence schemes and possible implications for policy. Results show that the systems analysed are rather effective in implementing the changes that they are designed to make, especially in the case of life-threatening working conditions, child labour, and corruption. However, the risk categories addressed by these projects are dictated by downstream expectations and do not necessarily correspond to the demands of the miners they are designed to protect. For instance, price calculation and income as well as gender considerations are particularly salient aspects and are not captured by RS programs, but are part of the S-LCA framework. This study confirms the need to support ASM formalisation and its inclusion in global supply chains, discusses the challenges of developing and upscaling RS programs for cobalt, and contributes to the definition of a set of categories to be scrutinized for the assessment and monitoring of these programs.

papergraph.title: Will reshoring manufacturing of advanced electric vehicle battery support renewable energy transition and climate targets?
papergraph.citation: Apoorv Lal et al. (2023) Will reshoring manufacturing of advanced electric vehicle battery support renewable energy transition and climate targets? doi:10.1126/sciadv.adg6740
papergraph.abstract: Recent global logistics and geopolitical challenges draw attention to the potential raw material shortages for electric vehicle (EV) batteries. Here, we analyze the long-term energy and sustainability prospects to ensure a secure and resilient midstream and downstream value chain for the U.S. EV battery market amid uncertain market expansion and evolving battery technologies. With current battery technologies, reshoring and ally-shoring the midstream and downstream EV battery manufacturing will reduce the carbon footprint by 15% and energy use by 5 to 7%. While next-generation cobalt-free battery technologies will achieve up to 27% carbon emission reduction, transitioning to 54% less carbon-intensive blade lithium iron phosphate may diminish the mitigation benefits of supply chain restructuring. Our findings underscore the importance of adopting nickel from secondary sources and nickel-rich ores. However, the advantages of restructuring the U.S. EV battery supply chain depend on projected battery technology advancements.

papergraph.title: Simultaneous Stochastic Optimization of Mining Complexes and Mineral Value Chains
papergraph.citation: R. Goodfellow et al. (2017) Simultaneous Stochastic Optimization of Mining Complexes and Mineral Value Chains doi:10.1007/s11004-017-9680-3
papergraph.abstract: Recent developments in modelling and optimization approaches for the production of mineral and energy resources have resulted in new simultaneous stochastic optimization frameworks and related digital technologies. A mining complex is a type of value chain whereby raw materials (minerals) extracted from various mineral deposits are transformed into a set of sellable products, using the available processing streams. The supply of materials extracted from a group of mines represents a major source of uncertainty in mining operations and mineral value chains. The simultaneous stochastic optimization of mining complexes, presented herein, aims to address major limitations of past approaches by modelling and optimizing several interrelated aspects of the mineral value chain in a single model. This single optimization model integrates material extraction from a set of sources along with their uncertainty, the related risk management, blending, stockpiling, non-linear transformations that occur in the available processing streams, the utilization of processing streams, and, finally, the transportation of products to customers. Uncertainty in materials extracted from the related mineral deposits of a mining complex is represented by a group of stochastic simulations. This paper presents a two-stage stochastic mixed integer nonlinear programming formulation for modelling and optimizing a mining complex, along with a metaheuristic-based solver that facilitates the practical optimization of exceptionally large mathematical formulations. The distinct advantages of the approach presented herein are demonstrated through two case studies, where the stochastic framework is compared to past approaches that ignore uncertainty. Results demonstrate major improvements in both meeting forecasted production targets and net present value. Concepts and methods presented in this paper for the simultaneous stochastic optimization for mining complexes may be adopted and applied to the optimization of smart oil fields.

papergraph.title: AI-Driven Optimization of Supply Chain and Logistics in Mechanical Engineering
papergraph.citation: Dipak Mahat et al. (2023) AI-Driven Optimization of Supply Chain and Logistics in Mechanical Engineering doi:10.1109/UPCON59197.2023.10434905
papergraph.venue: IEEE Uttar Pradesh Section International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Electronics Engineering
papergraph.abstract: Mechanical engineering businesses rely heavily on effective supply chain and logistics management to increase their productivity, efficiency, and competitiveness. Recent years have seen the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) approaches as potent instruments for improving logistics and supply chain operations. This abstract gives a thorough introduction to Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), an AI method inspired by nature, and how it may be used to improve mechanical engineering's supply chain and logistics. The foraging strategies of ants served as inspiration for the development of Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), a metaheuristic algorithm. It has garnered a lot of interest as a useful tool for supply chain and logistics optimization because of its capacity to tackle difficult optimization challenges. Inventory management, transportation routing, production scheduling, and demand forecasting are just some of the mechanical engineering problems that may be tackled with the help of ACO. Inventory optimization is a key use case for ACO in the context of mechanical engineering supply chain management. By modeling how ants locate food sources, ACO is able to ascertain optimum stock levels. To cut down on carrying costs and stockouts, it helps find the sweet spot between overstocking and understocking of raw materials and finished goods. Likewise, transportation route optimization is greatly aided by ACO. Transporting both inputs and outputs quickly and cheaply is crucial for factories. Taking into account variables like traffic, fuel prices, and delivery windows, ACO can determine the most efficient routes for trucks. This not only improves customer satisfaction through on-time deliveries but also decreases transportation expenses. Mechanical engineers may also use ACO to enhance production scheduling. Algorithms for Achieving Maximum Efficiency (ACOs) may plan the flow of production such that downtime, wasted materials, and lost revenue are kept to a minimum. Mechanical engineering firms may boost output and shorten manufacturing times by optimizing their production plans. Despite the inherent uncertainty in demand forecasting, ACO can improve prediction accuracy. Algorithms for adaptive costing and optimization (ACO) can aid mechanical engineering companies in making better judgments on production volumes and inventory levels by assessing past demand data and continuously revising forecasts based on real-time information. Overproduction and underproduction are avoided, resulting in cost savings and better service to customers. ACO may also be utilized to improve the process of finding and working with vendors. It may take into account several criteria, including supplier dependability, cost, and turnaround time, to select the most suitable vendors for mechanical engineering businesses. In addition to lowering material acquisition costs, this also guarantees a steady supply of high-quality raw materials. In conclusion, ACO-driven AI optimization of mechanical engineering's supply chain and logistics has several advantages, including lower costs, more efficiency, and happier clients. Companies in the mechanical engineering sector can gain an edge by implementing ACO algorithms into their inventory management, transportation routing, production scheduling, demand forecasting, and supplier selection processes. To remain competitive and resilient in the ever-changing area of mechanical engineering, the use of AI techniques like ACO will become increasingly vital as technology progresses.

papergraph.title: Digital Transformation and Data-Driven Management in a Regional Seafood Supply Chain: A Case Study in Kerala
papergraph.citation: A. Thasni et al. (2025) Digital Transformation and Data-Driven Management in a Regional Seafood Supply Chain: A Case Study in Kerala doi:10.1109/ACIT65614.2025.11185904
papergraph.abstract: This study examines the transformative impact of digital technologies and supply chain management innovations on the optimal functioning of regional seafood supply chains, using FreshToHome, a company rooted in Kerala, a southern state of India, as a focal case. In response to increasing demands for quality, traceability, and operational efficiency, the company employs an AI-based electronic auction system to procure raw materials directly from local fishermen. This technology-driven procurement strategy ensures consistent quality, minimizes reliance on manual intermediaries, and enhances transparency across the supply chain. Drawing on recent research in digital capabilities, smart supply chain systems, and real-time logistics optimization, this paper explores how the company leverages artificial intelligence and data integration to streamline sourcing, logistics operations, and last-mile fulfilment. The findings underscore how intelligent supply chain strategies reduce inefficiencies, foster consumer trust, and contribute to a sustainable competitive advantage. Ultimately, this study highlights the critical role of digitally transformed supply chain management in building agile, resilient, and scalable seafood supply chains, offering broader implications for food system modernization.

papergraph.title: Recent Advancements in Artificial Intelligence in Battery Recycling
papergraph.citation: Subin Antony Jose et al. (2024) Recent Advancements in Artificial Intelligence in Battery Recycling doi:10.3390/batteries10120440
papergraph.abstract: Battery recycling has become increasingly crucial in mitigating environmental pollution and conserving valuable resources. As demand for battery-powered devices rises across industries like automotive, electronics, and renewable energy, efficient recycling is essential. Traditional recycling methods, often reliant on manual labor, suffer from inefficiencies and environmental harm. However, recent artificial intelligence (AI) advancements offer promising solutions to these challenges. This paper reviews the latest developments in AI applications for battery recycling, focusing on methodologies, challenges, and future directions. AI technologies, particularly machine learning and deep learning models, are revolutionizing battery sorting, classification, and disassembly processes. AI-powered systems enhance efficiency by automating tasks such as battery identification, material characterization, and robotic disassembly, reducing human error and occupational hazards. Additionally, integrating AI with advanced sensing technologies like computer vision, spectroscopy, and X-ray imaging allows for precise material characterization and real-time monitoring, optimizing recycling strategies and material recovery rates. Despite these advancements, data quality, scalability, and regulatory compliance must be addressed to realize AI’s full potential in battery recycling. Collaborative efforts across interdisciplinary domains are essential to develop robust, scalable AI-driven recycling solutions, paving the way for a sustainable, circular economy in battery materials.

papergraph.title: Efficient Recycling Processes for Lithium-Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Sabyasachi Paul et al. (2025) Efficient Recycling Processes for Lithium-Ion Batteries doi:10.3390/ma18030613
papergraph.abstract: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are an indispensable power source for electric vehicles, portable electronics, and renewable energy storage systems due to their high energy density and long cycle life. However, the exponential growth in production and usage has necessitated highly effective recycling of end-of-life LIBs to recover valuable resources and minimize the environmental impact. Pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical processes are the most common recycling methods but pose considerable difficulties. The energy-intensive pyrometallurgical recycling process results in the loss of critical materials such as lithium and suffers from substantial emissions and high costs. Solvent extraction, a hydrometallurgical method, offers energy-efficient recovery for lithium, cobalt, and nickel but requires hazardous chemicals and careful waste management. Direct recycling is an alternative to traditional methods as it preserves the cathode active material (CAM) structure for quicker and cheaper regeneration. It also offers environmental advantages of lower energy intensity and chemical use. Hybrid pathways, combining hydrometallurgical and direct recycling methods, provide a cost-effective, scalable solution for LIB recycling, maximizing material recovery with minimal waste and environmental risk. The success of recycling methods depends on factors such as battery chemistry, the scalability of recovery processes, and the cost-effectiveness of waste material recovery. Though pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical processes have secured their position in LIB recycling, research is proceeding toward newer approaches, such as direct and hybrid methods. These alternatives are more efficient both environmentally and in terms of cost with a broader perspective into the future. In this review, we describe the current state of direct recycling as an alternative to traditional pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical methods for recuperating these critical materials, particularly lithium. We also highlight some significant advancements that make these objectives possible. As research progresses, direct recycling and its variations hold great potential to reshape the way LIBs are recycled, providing a sustainable pathway for battery material recovery and reuse.

papergraph.title: Sustainable Hydrometallurgical LFP Battery Recycling: Electrochemical Approaches
papergraph.citation: Cody B. van Beek et al. (2025) Sustainable Hydrometallurgical LFP Battery Recycling: Electrochemical Approaches doi:10.1002/cssc.202402699
papergraph.abstract: Abstract Lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs) are crucial for the energy transition, particularly with the rising demand for electric vehicles. Among different battery technologies, lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries have been attracting considerable attention in recent years due to their safe chemistry and relatively cheaper and abundant material composition. As LFP manufacturing is set to increase significantly, a proper end‐of‐life treatment of these batteries becomes essential to achieve circularity and minimize environmental impacts. However, recycling of LFP batteries is economically challenging because they do not contain many valuable transition metals. This Concept article focuses on recycling of LFP batteries, and explores whether economically viable LFP recycling can be made possible via improvement of recycling processes. Currently, hydrometallurgical recycling processes with inexpensive oxidants for leaching valuable lithium show potential, compared to pyrometallurgical processes. However, these processes still consume large amounts of chemicals. Electrochemical recycling methods that do not require continuous addition of external reagents, or minimize waste production, could lead to more sustainable and economically viable solutions for LFP battery recycling. In addition, combining these processes with other sustainable electrochemical technologies such as green hydrogen production, brine desalination and chemical production is a promising strategy to increase overall energy and product efficiency.

papergraph.title: Integrating pyro- and biohydrometallurgy in a green closed-loop lithium-ion battery recycling approach
papergraph.citation: Lukas Wiszniewski et al. (2025) Integrating pyro- and biohydrometallurgy in a green closed-loop lithium-ion battery recycling approach doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.116811
papergraph.abstract: State-of-the-art stand-alone recycling routes for lithium-ion batteries (LIB), such as pyrometallurgy or hydrometallurgy, face significant challenges, including high energy consumption, loss of valuable elements like lithium (Li) and susceptibility to a waste stream with varying cathode chemistry. The present work investigates the comparison of recovery targets when processing synthesized black mass between a standalone biohydrometallurgical process and a combined method, including an upstream pyrometallurgical process. In this approach, black mass undergoes carbothermic reduction in the InduMelt reactor, in which volatile elements like Li are vaporized and extracted via the gas stream, producing a Li-free alloy. Thermodynamic equilibrium calculations using FactSage TM showed the possibility of partial oxidation when using the alloy in an open-loop approach. Within a closed-loop approach, the alloy was pulverized for downstream biohydrometallurgy. Bioleaching experiments using adapted enriched cultures with synthetic pre-and untreated NMC811 and LFP black mass were performed at pulp densities (w/v) of 1 % and 10 %. The highest leaching efficiency of up to 100 % for Nickel (Ni), Manganese (Mn), Cobalt (Co), and Aluminium (Al) was achieved in the 1 % pre-treated experiment. Increasing the pulp density to 10 % reduced the leaching efficiency of these metals to less than 25% which could be attributed to factors such as the alkaline nature of the black mass, microbial inhibition and passivation due to precipitation. Pyrometallurgical pre-treatment improved metal leaching from NMC by up to 90 %, but had no impact on LFP. To close the materials loop, selective precipitation was applied.

papergraph.title: Efficient recycling of spent Li-ion battery cathodes by laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock
papergraph.citation: Ning Cao et al. (2025) Efficient recycling of spent Li-ion battery cathodes by laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock doi:10.1038/s41467-025-62434-1
papergraph.abstract: Environmental concerns over heavy metals in spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), coupled with the growing scarcity of metal resources, have heightened the need for efficient and cost-effective recycling of metals from spent cathodes. In this study, we propose a laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock strategy that rapidly separates cathode materials from Al foil in just seconds. The instantaneous thermal shock facilitates the reduction of refractory metal oxides and the decomposition of impurity layer, while simultaneously increasing the specific surface area and generating oxygen vacancies. This significantly enhances both the thermodynamics and kinetics of the subsequent leaching process. Leaching tests with 0.1 M HCl show that the efficiencies for Co, Ni, and Mn increase by 147.5%, 125.6%, and 140.0%, respectively, compared to untreated materials. Over 97% of the metals are recovered even using 0.5 M HCl. Here, we propose a laser strategy that is more economically viable and significantly reduces the environmental impact. For rapid and efficient recovery of spent cathodes, this study shows a laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock strategy that separates cathode materials from Al foil within seconds and enhances the thermodynamics and kinetics of metal leaching.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.

papergraph.title: Emerging Processes for Sustainable Li-Ion Battery Cathode Recycling
papergraph.citation: Sohini Bhattacharyya et al. (2024) Emerging Processes for Sustainable Li-Ion Battery Cathode Recycling. doi:10.1002/smll.202400557
papergraph.abstract: The colossal growth in the use of Li-ion batteries (LiBs) has raised serious concerns over the supply chain of strategic minerals, e.g., Co, Ni, and Li, that make up the cathode active materials (CAM). Recycling spent LiBs is an important step toward sustainability that can establish a circular economy by effectively tackling large amounts of e-waste while ensuring an unhindered supply of critical minerals. Among the various methods of LiB recycling available, pyro- and hydrometallurgy have been utilized in the industry owing to their ease of operation and high efficiency, although they are associated with significant environmental concerns. Direct recycling, a more recent concept that aims to relithiate spent LiBs without disrupting the lattice structure of the CAMs, has been realized only in the laboratory scale so far and further optimization is required before it can be extended to the bulk scale. Additionally, significant progress has been made in the areas of hydrometallurgy in terms of using ecofriendly green lixiviants and alternate sources of energy, e.g., microwave and electrochemical, that makes the recycling processes more efficient and sustainable. In this review, the latest developments in LiB recycling are discussed that have focused on environmental and economic viability, as well as process intensification. These include deep eutectic solvent based recycling, electrochemical and microwave-assisted recycling, and various types of direct recycling.
papergraph.benchmark: Environmental and economic viability, process intensification
papergraph.contribution: Discussion of the latest developments in LiB recycling focusing on sustainability and efficiency
papergraph.limitation: Direct recycling is currently limited to laboratory scale and requires further optimization for bulk scale application

papergraph.title: A study of the techno-economic feasibility of H2-based energy storage systems in remote areas
papergraph.citation: P. Marocco et al. (2020) A study of the techno-economic feasibility of H2-based energy storage systems in remote areas doi:10.1016/j.enconman.2020.112768
papergraph.abstract: Abstract The development of efficient and sustainable energy solutions and the attempt to reduce carbon dioxide emissions are leading to an increasing penetration of Renewable Energy Sources (RES). Effective Electrical Energy Storage (EES) solutions need therefore to be developed to deal with the issue of fitting locally available RES and loads. Hydrogen can become an interesting option because of its high energy density, long-term storage capability and modularity. In particular, in isolated micro-grid and off-grid remote areas, intermittent RES integrated with H2-based storage systems can allow to lower, or even eliminate, the usage of diesel engines and avoid the need for expensive and invasive grid connections. The present study is part of the European REMOTE project, whose main goal is to prove the added value of H2-based energy storage solutions with respect to alternative technologies in terms of economics, technical and environmental benefits. Four demonstration sites supplied by renewable electricity will be installed in either isolated micro-grids or off-grid remote areas throughout all Europe, from Italy (two sites) and Greece to Norway. The aim of this work is to perform a techno-economic analysis and demonstrate the effectiveness of the hybrid H2-battery Power-To-Power (P2P) solution in reducing the usage of external sources (e.g., diesel engines or grid) in a cost-effective way, with different load and environment conditions. The economic viability of the considered scenarios was outlined by computing the Levelized Cost Of Energy (LCOE). For each of the four sites, the innovative renewable configuration was compared with the current/alternative one. The REMOTE project partners provided main input data for the analysis: techno-economic data from the technology suppliers, whereas electricity consumption and RES production values from the end users of the four isolated locations. LCOE values derived using cost inputs both from REMOTE and literature are presented for a comparison. Results from the energy simulations revealed that the need for an external source is significantly reduced thanks to RES together with the hybrid storage system. Moreover, for all the four sites the renewable solution was shown to be more profitable than the current or alternative one, either in the short term or in the longer term.

papergraph.title: Optimal Sizing, Techno-Economic Feasibility and Reliability Analysis of Hybrid Renewable Energy System: A Systematic Review of Energy Storage Systems’ Integration
papergraph.citation: A. A. Khan et al. (2025) Optimal Sizing, Techno-Economic Feasibility and Reliability Analysis of Hybrid Renewable Energy System: A Systematic Review of Energy Storage Systems’ Integration doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3535520
papergraph.abstract: One of the most significant ways to improve energy reliability and lessen reliance on fossil fuels is to combine renewable energy sources with energy storage systems. Using wind, solar, and battery storage as case studies, the article examines hybrid renewable energy system (HRES) size, optimization, techno-economic potential, and reliability in extensive detail. In order to minimize expenses and emissions while satisfying energy demands, the sizing process consists of evaluating the appropriate capacity of each component. In order to tackle the growing demand with a low carbon footprint, this study investigates the prospect of leveraging hybrid renewable energy (HRE) storage. System demands, budget, and performance indicators are some of the most critical considerations when selecting an energy storage system (ESS) for a renewable energy system. Whether or not the storage option is appropriate for HRE systems depends on the setup requirements. The selection of an ESS technology necessitates taking into account aspects like energy and power requirements, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, versatility, and reliability. The investigation also considers factors such as electricity long-term viability, dependability, technical and financial feasibility, and ecological sustainability when evaluating hybrid renewable energy power-producing technologies. Improving dependability and smoothing down power output are two major benefits of ESS integration. The findings show that integrating HRES with ESS can lead to more sustainable energy systems, providing a long-term, reliable, and cost-effective solution. Findings emphasize the need for further study of optimization methods, meta-heuristic algorithm strategies, system components, design constraints, and desired techniques.

papergraph.title: Toward Sustainable and Clean Energy Futures: A Techno-Economic Review of Solar PV Systems, Challenges, and Opportunities
papergraph.citation: Riad Mollik Babu et al. (2025) Toward Sustainable and Clean Energy Futures: A Techno-Economic Review of Solar PV Systems, Challenges, and Opportunities doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3614771
papergraph.abstract: The integration of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems has gained significant attention from the global community, aiming to reduce dependency on fossil fuels and combat climate change. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the latest technical advancements and economic analyses of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems across various scales and applications, encompassing both grid-connected and off-grid configurations. The cost-effectiveness of important innovations, like enhanced inverter technologies, high-efficiency cell designs, and energy storage system integration, is summarized. Across a range of climatic and regional contexts, the review critically assesses economic parameters, including financial feasibility, the levelized cost of energy (LCOE), payback period, and return on investment (ROI). Along with examining intermittency issues, high startup costs, and new solutions, it also reviews the significant technical and financial challenges associated with voltage and frequency regulation in power systems with high PV penetration. To comprehend their role in promoting solar PV deployment worldwide, the environmental effects and policy framework, including government incentives and innovative financial models, are examined. Different case studies from both developing and developed countries are included to highlight the intersection among technologies, economics, and policies. This review identifies critical gaps in solar PV integration and provides comprehensive guidelines for researchers, investors, and policymakers with the ultimate goal of promoting clean energy systems to achieve sustainable development goals.

papergraph.title: Techno-Economic and Environmental Analysis of Solar PV System at Sher-e-Bangla National Cricket Stadium: A Comprehensive Case Study
papergraph.citation: Mohammad Ariful Islam Rafi et al. (2025) Techno-Economic and Environmental Analysis of Solar PV System at Sher-e-Bangla National Cricket Stadium: A Comprehensive Case Study doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3553636
papergraph.abstract: The proposed rooftop solar photovoltaic (PV) system at the Sher-e-Bangla National Cricket Stadium (SBNCS) demonstrates a sustainable energy solution addressing Bangladesh’s energy challenges. The system has a capacity of 83.2 kWp and is estimated to generate 129.5 MWh of energy annually. This deployment reduces reliance on fossil fuels and contributes to global Sustainable Development Goal 7 (SDG7). The performance evaluation reveals a Performance Ratio (PR) of 79.4%, ensuring efficient solar resource utilization. Economically, the project involves a total investment of $101,031, achieving annual energy cost savings of $5,370. Financial feasibility metrics include a Net Present Value (NPV) of $99,131.5, an Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of 6%, and a Payback Period (PBP) of 13 years. Furthermore, the system reduces 50 tons of CO2 emissions annually, resulting in a Social Cost of Carbon (SCC) savings of $77,064 over its 25-year lifespan. The project’s Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) is calculated as $0.03/kWh, reflecting its long-term cost-effectiveness. This analysis highlights the economic, environmental, and performance benefits of implementing a rooftop solar PV system at SBNCS, offering a scalable model for integrating renewable energy into and other stadiums and large infrastructure. This study can aid in the integration of renewable energy into the grid and assist policymakers in facilitating the future energy storage systems and expanding grid-tied operations for enhanced sustainability.

papergraph.title: Evaluating the feasibility of geothermal deep direct-use in the United States
papergraph.citation: K. Beckers et al. (2021) Evaluating the feasibility of geothermal deep direct-use in the United States doi:10.1016/J.ENCONMAN.2021.114335
papergraph.abstract: Abstract This paper investigates the techno-economic feasibility of geothermal deep direct-use for heating, cooling, and thermal energy storage in the United States. The six 2017–2019 deep direct-use projects funded by the U.S. Department of Energy are reviewed and evaluated using the simulation tool GEOPHIRES, and results are compared with prior studies, existing geothermal district heating systems in Europe and the United States, as well as non-geothermal centralized and non-centralized heating, cooling, and thermal energy storage systems. Analysis indicates that deep direct-use feasibility varies widely, depending on subsurface characteristics, system design, and financial conditions. Project base case levelized cost of heat values ranged between $13 and $350/MWh; key drivers lowering the levelized cost of heat include higher reservoir temperatures, shallower reservoir depths, higher well flow rates, higher utilization rates, lower drilling costs, and lower discount rates. Incentives such as grants and an investment tax credit resulted in small improvements in project economics. Base case levelized cost of heat values for four projects fell within the range of levelized cost values for existing systems in Europe and the United States, and were comparable to values found in previous studies, including the 2019 GeoVision study. The lowest-cost projects for district heating had comparable levelized cost of heat values to existing fossil-fuel-driven district heating systems, and lower values than decentralized heating with natural gas boilers or heat pumps. Chilled water production with absorption chillers driven by a high-quality geothermal resource obtained attractive levelized cost of cooling values, in line with values from typical centralized cooling production facilities and lower than domestic decentralized cooling with air-conditioning units. Also, thermal energy storage is a potential deep direct-use application, where levelized cost of storage values can be obtained that are comparable to those of other thermal storage techniques such as borehole thermal energy storage and hot water storage tanks. Other aspects of deep direct-use, which are not captured in a standard levelized cost of energy metric, include the ability to decarbonize the heating and cooling supply, and provide reliability and resiliency to the energy infrastructure. These attributes are gaining increased attention and improve overall project feasibility. Finally, this paper identifies technical, market, policy, and social barriers for deep direct-use development in the United States, and provides potential approaches for reducing these barriers.

papergraph.title: Seasonal Energy Storage to Support South Africa’s Energy Transition
papergraph.citation: Tiaan Vivier et al. (2025) Seasonal Energy Storage to Support South Africa’s Energy Transition doi:10.1109/ICCEP65222.2025.11143783
papergraph.venue: International Conference on Clean Electrical Power
papergraph.abstract: Variable renewable energy (VRE), in the form of wind and solar power, is poised to be the foundation of South Africa’s future decarbonized electrical power system. However, the intermittent nature of VRE poses significant challenges to reliability in this power system and is likely to result in seasonal energy imbalances. Seasonal energy storage (SES) offers a potential solution by storing excess energy generated during periods of high renewable output and releasing it when generation is low, effectively bridging seasonal gaps. This study investigates SES's technical feasibility and financial viability in South Africa's future decarbonized power system. By analyzing the country's future energy mix, this study assesses the optimal timing for deploying SES. A techno-economic analysis is performed incorporating three different SES technologies (pumped hydro, compressed air, and hydrogen energy storage) and different discharge durations (one week, two weeks and four weeks) to determine which technology and sizing characteristics are more suited for SES in South Africa, using the benefit-cost ratio (BCR) metric. The results show that, for the reference scenario in South Africa’s most recent capacity expansion plan, SES will be financially viable by approximately 2036 for a one-week discharge duration and by 2039 for a two-week discharge duration, which occur at VRE penetration levels of 48% and 54%, respectively. Pumped hydro energy storage with a two-week discharge duration emerges as the most financially viable SES. Under current technology costs, hydrogen energy storage only emerges as the most viable option towards 2060, but decreasing costs could accelerate its financial viability to the mid-2040s. The 2050 energy mix with added SES shows significant reductions in VRE curtailment compared to the non-SES scenario. This research provides valuable insights for South African and international stakeholders involved with optimizing future power systems related to the value of SES.

keynotes.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics

keynote.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics
keynote.core_problem: Sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) face challenges in terms of irreversible phase transformations during cycling, poor air stability, complex charge-compensation mechanisms, and relatively high cost compared to LiFePO4-based lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). These issues hinder their commercialization and large-scale implementation.
contributions: Techno-economic analysis of NaxTMO2 cathode materials
Investigation into the degradation mechanism of NaxTMO2 upon cycling and air exposure
Development of strategies to improve the electrochemical performance and air stability of NaxTMO2
limitations: Irreversible phase transformations during cycling
Poor air stability
Complex charge-compensation mechanisms
Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs

gap_signals.text: Complex charge-compensation mechanisms
Irreversible phase transformations during cycling
Poor air stability
Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs

Discussion on the remaining challenges and new opportunities concerning the practical applications of NaxTMO2, with an emphasis on the cost, large-scale fabrication capability, and electrochemical performance.
Correlating structure–performance relationships and establishing general design strategies of NaxTMO2 must be considered for the commercialization of NIBs.

important_claims.claim: Sodium layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2) are important for reducing costs, increasing energy density and cycling stability, and improving the safety of NIBs for large-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: The advantages of NIBs include abundant sodium resources and the use of inexpensive, noncritical transition metals like manganese and iron, which reduce the cost of raw materials.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions for NaxTMO2 cathode materials, making it valuable for researchers focusing on the development and commercialization of NIBs.

keynotes.title: Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling in the Circular Economy: A Review

keynote.title: Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling in the Circular Economy: A Review
keynote.core_problem: The need for effective recycling and material recovery of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to support a circular economy, with a focus on the lack of comprehensive reviews that integrate both recycling methods and circular economy challenges.
contributions: A comprehensive review of 93 articles (66 original research articles and 27 review articles) on LIB recycling and circular economy.

Identification of key areas requiring further research, such as techno-economic assessment, reverse logistics, global EV material recovery potential, lifecycle assessment, and circular business models.
methods: Literature review using the Web of Science core collection database.
Categorization of studies into experiments, evaluation, and planning.
experiments_or_evidence: Exponential increase in publications related to LIB recycling and recovery.
Pre-treatment processes are widely discussed, particularly in hydrometallurgy and direct physical recycling (DPR).
assumptions: Recycling is a key method for recovering materials from spent LIBs.
The circular economy framework is essential for sustainable management of LIBs.
limitations: Policy and regulatory affairs have received less attention compared to recycling methods.
Techno-economic assessments, safe reverse logistics, and lifecycle assessments of recycling processes require more focus.

gap_signals.text: Policy and regulatory affairs have received less attention compared to recycling methods.
Techno-economic assessments, safe reverse logistics, and lifecycle assessments of recycling processes require more focus.
Further research is needed on the techno-economic assessment of the recycling process, safe reverse logistics, and lifecycle assessment of experiments processes.
More attention should be given to the application of circular business models, stakeholder engagement, and clear policy guidelines.

datasets_or_materials: Web of Science core collection database

important_claims.claim: Publications in the area of LIB recycling and circular economy are increasing exponentially.
important_claims.evidence: Review of 93 articles from the Web of Science core collection database.

important_claims.claim: Direct physical recycling (DPR) is a promising technique that requires further attention.
important_claims.evidence: Widespread discussion of pre-treatment processes in the reviewed literature.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a structured overview of the current state of research on LIB recycling and the circular economy, highlighting key areas for future investigation. It can serve as a foundational resource for researchers and policymakers looking to address the gaps identified in the study.

keynotes.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries

keynote.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The escalating demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has created supply chain issues, and there is a need to explore alternatives such as sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) for grid storage and automotive applications. However, SIBs face significant hurdles in terms of performance, stability, and cost-effectiveness.
contributions: Identify the critical challenges in making SIBs viable from both chemical and techno-economic perspectives.
Provide a comparison of materials chemistry, working mechanisms, and cost between mainstream LIB systems and prospective SIB systems.
Discuss intrinsic challenges of SIBs related to storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, calendar life, and safe operation.
Illustrate issues related to the scalability of material production, materials engineering feasibility, and energy-dense electrode design and fabrication.
List and discuss promising pathways toward achieving high-energy-density, stable, and cost-effective SIBs.

gap_signals.text: Significant hurdles in the commercialization of SIBs, particularly for grid storage and automotive applications, need to be addressed.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are emerging as a promising alternative to LIBs due to the high abundance and low cost of sodium and other raw materials.
important_claims.evidence: The article discusses the potential of SIBs as an alternative to LIBs, highlighting the advantages of sodium's abundance and lower costs.

important_claims.claim: Critical challenges in making SIBs viable include storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, calendar life, and safe operation.
important_claims.evidence: The paper outlines these intrinsic challenges that need to be overcome for SIBs to be commercially viable.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This perspective provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions for SIBs, which can guide further research and development efforts in this field.

keynotes.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications

keynote.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications
keynote.core_problem: The need for effective, scalable, and sustainable energy storage solutions due to the increasing demand from electric vehicles, portable devices, and renewable energy sources, while also addressing the limited supply and high costs of lithium resources.
contributions: Thorough analysis of Li-ion and Na-ion electrochemical systems, including fundamental concepts, recent developments, and new challenges.
Comparison of energy density, rate capability, cycle stability, environmental impact, and cost-efficiency between Li-ion and Na-ion systems.
Discussion on the commercialization and scaling prospects of next-generation batteries, with a focus on recycling, green synthesis, and smart grid integration.
Evaluation of the environmental and techno-economic effects of transitioning from lithium to sodium-based chemistry.
methods: Literature review and analysis of existing research on Li-ion and Na-ion battery technologies.

gap_signals.text: Pinpointing technological gaps in current Li-ion and Na-ion battery technologies.

important_claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries have dominated the market due to their long cycle life, high energy density, and established manufacturing infrastructure.
important_claims.evidence: The paper highlights the dominance of Li-ion batteries in the energy storage market, attributed to their performance and industrial setup.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries are emerging as a promising alternative due to the abundance and lower cost of sodium compared to lithium.
important_claims.evidence: The paper discusses the advantages of using sodium, which is cheaper and more abundant than lithium, making Na-ion batteries a viable alternative.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions of Li-ion and Na-ion battery technologies, useful for researchers looking to address the identified gaps and develop sustainable, high-performance energy storage solutions.

keynotes.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The need for high-performance, resource-abundant cathode materials for lithium-ion batteries to support sustainable energy storage systems.
contributions: Design and electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions
Achievement of stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations
Demonstration of high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages
Projection of commercial competitiveness through techno-economic analysis
methods: Systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess
Modifications to the cathode material composition
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxides
Cycling performance testing in full-cell configurations
Capacity retention tests at practical loadings and voltages
Techno-economic analysis for commercial potential

gap_signals.text: Details on specific modifications and their impact on performance are not provided.

important_claims.claim: Mn-rich layered oxides offer high specific capacities and favorable sustainability.
important_claims.evidence: Not explicitly provided in the given text, but implied by the focus on these materials.

important_claims.claim: Selected compositions deliver high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages.
important_claims.evidence: Reported in the abstract, but no specific data or experimental details are provided.

important_claims.claim: Techno-economic analysis supports the commercial potential of these materials.
important_claims.evidence: Mentioned in the abstract, but no specific results or methodology are detailed.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The work provides a foundation for further research into the optimization of Mn-rich layered oxides as cathode materials, particularly in terms of their composition and performance under various conditions. The findings also highlight the importance of considering both technical and economic factors in the development of new battery materials.

keynotes.title: Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass

keynote.title: Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass
keynote.core_problem: The need for energy storage at grid scale due to the growth of intermittent electricity generation from solar PV and wind, and the economic comparison between different systems to meet this demand.
contributions: A techno-economic model to calculate the levelised cost of electricity (LCOE) for two systems: solar PV and battery storage, and solar PV with combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas from biomass.
Analysis of parameters that most impact the LCOE for each system.
methods: Construction of a techno-economic model to calculate LCOE under Queensland, Australia conditions.
Assumption of a variable energy demand curve and specific costs for lithium-ion batteries and solar PV installations.
experiments_or_evidence: Calculation of LCOE for a 240 MWh/d project capacity for both systems.
Forecasting future LCOE based on projected installed costs.
assumptions: Use of lithium-ion batteries and costs derived from the Homsdale Power Reserve in South Australia.
limitations: The analysis is specific to conditions in Queensland, Australia.
The solar PV and bio-crude system is considered a niche solution, limited to areas with substantial biomass resources.

gap_signals.text: The solar PV and bio-crude system is considered a niche solution, limited to areas with substantial biomass resources.
The analysis is specific to conditions in Queensland, Australia.
Development of larger scale bioenergy value chains, with the longer term goal of converting lignocellulosic biomass materials into renewable transportation fuels and chemicals.

datasets_or_materials: Cost data for lithium-ion batteries and solar PV installations.
code_or_implementation: No specific code or implementation details provided.

important_claims.claim: The integration of solar PV and the production and combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas using fast pyrolysis of biomass leads to competitively priced dispatchable renewable energy.
important_claims.evidence: Estimated LCOE for the solar PV and bio-crude system is 116 AUD/MWh, which is lower than the LCOE for the solar PV and battery system (170 AUD/MWh).

important_claims.claim: The combination of solar PV and bio-crude combustion leads to lower LCOEs than using bioenergy alone, due to the rapidly decreasing costs of large-scale solar PV.
important_claims.evidence: The LCOE for the solar PV and bio-crude system is estimated to be in the range of 104-125 AUD/MWh, making it cheaper than using solar PV and batteries.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The model and findings can be used as a basis for further research into integrating renewable energy sources and developing bioenergy value chains, particularly in regions with significant biomass resources.

keynotes.title: Polymer‐Based Solid‐State Electrolytes for High‐Energy‐Density Lithium‐Ion Batteries – Review

keynote.title: Polymer‐Based Solid‐State Electrolytes for High‐Energy‐Density Lithium‐Ion Batteries – Review
keynote.core_problem: The increasing demand for high-performance lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with high-energy-density, driven by the use of portable electronic devices and electric vehicles, necessitates the development of suitable electrolytes.
contributions: Comparison of intrinsic characteristics and Li+ conduction mechanisms of various electrolytes, emphasizing their suitability for high-energy-density LIBs.
Discussion on the advantages and disadvantages of commonly used polymer matrix materials in solid-state electrolytes (SSEs).
Review and analysis of interfacial issues such as cathode/electrolyte and anode/electrolyte interactions, and approaches to address these limitations.

gap_signals.text: Perspectives on future research directions for developing high-energy-density LIBs are highlighted building upon the existing literature.

important_claims.claim: Polymer-based solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) are the most promising candidates for high-energy-density LIBs due to their comprehensive properties.
important_claims.evidence: This work compares the intrinsic characteristics and Li+ conduction mechanisms of various electrolytes, aiming at emphasizing their suitability for high-energy-density LIBs. Among all electrolytes, polymer-based solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) are the most promising candidates, as they demonstrate the most comprehensive properties.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a detailed comparison of different types of electrolytes and focuses on the challenges and potential solutions for using polymer-based SSEs in high-energy-density LIBs, which can guide future research and development in this field.

keynotes.title: Challenges and the Way to Improve Lithium‐Ion Battery Technology for Next‐Generation Energy Storage

keynote.title: Challenges and the Way to Improve Lithium‐Ion Battery Technology for Next‐Generation Energy Storage
keynote.core_problem: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) face critical challenges, particularly in achieving higher energy density and long-term electrochemical stability, which limits their performance in high-power applications such as electric and hybrid electric vehicles.
contributions: Comprehensive analysis of fundamental limitations hindering LIBs from achieving superior energy density and long-term electrochemical stability
Discussion on challenges, emerging strategies, and future perspectives for cathode materials, anode materials, separators, and current collectors
Examination of innovative approaches in material architecture, electrode design optimizations, and advanced separators and current collectors

keynotes.title: Integrating pyro- and biohydrometallurgy in a green closed-loop lithium-ion battery recycling approach

keynote.title: Integrating pyro- and biohydrometallurgy in a green closed-loop lithium-ion battery recycling approach
keynote.core_problem: State-of-the-art stand-alone recycling routes for lithium-ion batteries (LIB) face significant challenges, including high energy consumption, loss of valuable elements like lithium (Li), and susceptibility to a waste stream with varying cathode chemistry.
contributions: Investigates the comparison of recovery targets when processing synthesized black mass between a standalone biohydrometallurgical process and a combined method, including an upstream pyrometallurgical process.
methods: Carbothermic reduction in the InduMelt reactor to vaporize and extract volatile elements like Li, producing a Li-free alloy.
Thermodynamic equilibrium calculations using FactSage TM to show the possibility of partial oxidation when using the alloy in an open-loop approach.
Pulverization of the alloy for downstream biohydrometallurgy in a closed-loop approach.
Bioleaching experiments using adapted enriched cultures with synthetic pre-and untreated NMC811 and LFP black mass at pulp densities of 1% and 10%.
experiments_or_evidence: The highest leaching efficiency of up to 100% for Nickel (Ni), Manganese (Mn), Cobalt (Co), and Aluminium (Al) was achieved in the 1% pre-treated experiment.

Increasing the pulp density to 10% reduced the leaching efficiency of these metals to less than 25%, attributed to factors such as the alkaline nature of the black mass, microbial inhibition, and passivation due to precipitation.
Pyrometallurgical pre-treatment improved metal leaching from NMC by up to 90% but had no impact on LFP.

gap_signals.text: Pyrometallurgical pre-treatment improved metal leaching from NMC but had no impact on LFP, indicating potential limitations in the effectiveness of the method for different types of LIBs.
Increasing the pulp density to 10% reduced the leaching efficiency of metals, suggesting that higher pulp densities may not be suitable without further optimization.

datasets_or_materials: Synthetic pre-and untreated NMC811 and LFP black mass

important_claims.claim: Pyrometallurgical pre-treatment improved metal leaching from NMC by up to 90%.
important_claims.evidence: Bioleaching experiments showed that the highest leaching efficiency of up to 100% for Ni, Mn, Co, and Al was achieved in the 1% pre-treated experiment, and pyrometallurgical pre-treatment specifically improved metal leaching from NMC.

important_claims.claim: Increasing the pulp density to 10% reduced the leaching efficiency of metals to less than 25%.
important_claims.evidence: Bioleaching experiments at 10% pulp density showed a significant reduction in leaching efficiency, which could be attributed to factors such as the alkaline nature of the black mass, microbial inhibition, and passivation due to precipitation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a foundation for integrating pyrometallurgical and biohydrometallurgical processes in a closed-loop approach for more efficient and sustainable LIB recycling. The findings can guide future research on optimizing conditions for different types of LIBs and improving the overall recovery of valuable elements.

keynotes.title: Efficient recycling of spent Li-ion battery cathodes by laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock

keynote.core_problem: The need for efficient and cost-effective recycling of metals from spent lithium-ion battery (LIB) cathodes, driven by environmental concerns over heavy metals and the scarcity of metal resources.
contributions: Proposed a laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock strategy to rapidly separate cathode materials from Al foil in seconds.
Enhanced the thermodynamics and kinetics of the subsequent leaching process, significantly increasing metal recovery efficiency.
methods: Laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock on spent LIB cathodes to separate them from Al foil.
Leaching tests with 0.1 M HCl and 0.5 M HCl to recover Co, Ni, and Mn from the treated cathode materials.
experiments_or_evidence: Leaching tests showed that the efficiencies for Co, Ni, and Mn increased by 147.5%, 125.6%, and 140.0%, respectively, compared to untreated materials.
Over 97% of the metals were recovered using 0.5 M HCl.

gap_signals.text: The proposed laser strategy is more economically viable and significantly reduces the environmental impact compared to traditional methods.

datasets_or_materials: 0.1 M HCl and 0.5 M HCl for leaching tests

important_claims.claim: The laser-induced high-temperature thermal shock strategy can rapidly separate cathode materials from Al foil in just seconds.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results show that the thermal shock facilitates the reduction of refractory metal oxides and the decomposition of the impurity layer, while increasing the specific surface area and generating oxygen vacancies.

important_claims.claim: The proposed method enhances the thermodynamics and kinetics of the subsequent leaching process, leading to higher metal recovery rates.
important_claims.evidence: Leaching tests with 0.1 M HCl showed significant increases in the recovery efficiencies for Co, Ni, and Mn, and over 97% of the metals were recovered even using 0.5 M HCl.

Hydrometallurgy
Direct recycling

gap_signals.text: Direct recycling is currently limited to laboratory scale and requires further optimization for bulk scale application.
Pyro- and hydrometallurgy have been utilized in the industry owing to their ease of operation and high efficiency, although they are associated with significant environmental concerns.

Direct recycling, a more recent concept that aims to relithiate spent LiBs without disrupting the lattice structure of the CAMs, has been realized only in the laboratory scale so far and further optimization is required before it can be extended to the bulk scale.

important_claims.claim: Significant progress has been made in the areas of hydrometallurgy in terms of using ecofriendly green lixiviants and alternate sources of energy, e.g., microwave and electrochemical, that makes the recycling processes more efficient and sustainable.
important_claims.evidence: These advancements include deep eutectic solvent based recycling, electrochemical and microwave-assisted recycling, and various types of direct recycling.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions of LiB recycling, highlighting key areas for further research and development, particularly in scaling up direct recycling and improving the sustainability of existing methods.

methods: Conducted energy simulations using data from the REMOTE project partners, including techno-economic data from technology suppliers and electricity consumption and RES production values from end users.
Calculated the Levelized Cost Of Energy (LCOE) for each site to assess economic viability.
experiments_or_evidence: Energy simulations revealed that the need for an external source is significantly reduced with the use of RES and the hybrid storage system.
For all four sites, the renewable solution was shown to be more profitable than the current or alternative one, either in the short term or in the longer term.
assumptions: The provided techno-economic data and electricity consumption and RES production values are accurate and representative of typical conditions.

keynotes.title: Seasonal Energy Storage to Support South Africa’s Energy Transition

keynote.title: Seasonal Energy Storage to Support South Africa’s Energy Transition
keynote.core_problem: The intermittent nature of variable renewable energy (VRE) like wind and solar power poses significant challenges to the reliability of South Africa's future decarbonized electrical power system, leading to potential seasonal energy imbalances.
contributions: Investigation into the technical feasibility and financial viability of seasonal energy storage (SES) in South Africa's future decarbonized power system.
Assessment of optimal timing for deploying SES based on the country's future energy mix.
Techno-economic analysis of three different SES technologies (pumped hydro, compressed air, and hydrogen energy storage) with varying discharge durations.
methods: Techno-economic analysis using the benefit-cost ratio (BCR) metric.
Analysis of South Africa's most recent capacity expansion plan to determine VRE penetration levels and SES deployment timing.
experiments_or_evidence: Financial viability of SES by approximately 2036 for a one-week discharge duration and by 2039 for a two-week discharge duration at VRE penetration levels of 48% and 54%, respectively.
Pumped hydro energy storage with a two-week discharge duration is identified as the most financially viable SES technology under current conditions.
Hydrogen energy storage becomes the most viable option towards 2060, but decreasing costs could make it viable by the mid-2040s.
Significant reductions in VRE curtailment in the 2050 energy mix with added SES compared to the non-SES scenario.

gap_signals.text: Decreasing costs of hydrogen energy storage could accelerate its financial viability to the mid-2040s.

datasets_or_materials: South Africa’s most recent capacity expansion plan

important_claims.claim: Pumped hydro energy storage with a two-week discharge duration is the most financially viable SES technology under current conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Results from the techno-economic analysis using the BCR metric.

important_claims.claim: SES will be financially viable by approximately 2036 for a one-week discharge duration and by 2039 for a two-week discharge duration.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis based on VRE penetration levels of 48% and 54% in South Africa's most recent capacity expansion plan.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides valuable insights for optimizing future power systems, particularly in the context of integrating SES to support high levels of VRE. The findings can inform policy decisions and investment strategies for energy storage technologies in South Africa and other regions facing similar challenges.

boxue_delegation_plans.goal: 不要泛泛讨论‘储能技术的未来’。围绕一个可证伪的科学问题：'电极材料（如钴酸锂、磷酸铁锂、硫正极）的固有化学稳定性、电解液分解电位、锂/钠离子迁移动力学以及固态电解质界面（SEI）的演化，是否共同决定了一个明确的能量密度上限（例如 < 500 Wh/kg）和循环寿命阈值（例如 < 10,000次，在80%容量保持率下），从而在技术和经济可行性上构成固态/液态电池的固有极限；该上限是否可以通过全新的化学体系（如锂-硫、锂-空气、钠离子）或梯度浓度/纳米结构工程被实质性突破？'

comparison.prompt_assign_task: implemented as persistent create_task DAG entries with assigned agent, phase, dependency, deliverable, priority, and acceptance criteria
comparison.prompt_review_output: implemented as reviewer_gate plus boxue_final dependency gates; Boxue can add revision tasks via the task system
comparison.prompt_synthesize: implemented as bianlun_synthesis, paperwriter_draft, and boxue_final synthesis tasks
comparison.prompt_adjust_plan: implemented operationally by creating additional tasks or new delegation plans after reviewing outputs
comparison.prompt_finalize: implemented as boxue_final decision task, not as specialist execution

stronger_than_old_pipeline_tasks: role-specific tasks instead of generic phase tasks
risk constraints embedded in every task
optional teammate spawning for unblocked specialist work
prompt_alignment.remaining_manual_gate: Boxue still needs to review outputs and decide whether to create revision tasks; this preserves human/lead control over shared project state.

coordination_policy.boxue_role: decompose, assign, review, synthesize, adjust, finalize
coordination_policy.specialist_role: execute only the assigned scientific subtask
coordination_policy.shared_state: state-changing PaperGraph/project updates should be gated by lead/synthesis tasks

tasks.title: Build grounded PaperGraph evidence substrate
acceptance_criteria: At least one verifiable source or an explicit retrieval-failure report
Method/scenario/benchmark/contribution/limitation fields are populated or flagged unknown
Unsupported claims are marked for human review

tasks.title: Rank high-value knowledge gaps
acceptance_criteria: Every reported gap has supporting references or is flagged as ungrounded

tasks.title: Generate and evolve gap-grounded hypotheses
acceptance_criteria: Each hypothesis links to a gap_id
Each hypothesis contains mechanism and falsification condition

tasks.title: Run Socratic critique
acceptance_criteria: At least one counterexample per hypothesis
At least one alternative explanation per hypothesis

tasks.title: Moderate structured debate and synthesize refined hypothesis
acceptance_criteria: Arguments are separated into factual vs conceptual disagreements
Refined hypothesis lists improvements from critique

boxue_delegation_plans.next_step: Let unblocked specialist tasks run first. Boxue should review outputs at dependency gates, then adjust or unlock downstream phases.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Review of molecular decomposition mechanisms and analysis of capacity fade rates
Categorization of capacity fade rates and recommendations for improved measurement methods for assessing molecular lifetimes

Abstract: Aqueous organic redox flow batteries (RFBs) could enable widespread integration of renewable energy, but only if costs are sufficiently low. Because the levelized cost of storage for an RFB is a function of electrolyte lifetime, understanding and improving the chemical stability of active....[truncated]
Full text excerpt:
Aqueous organic redox flow batteries (RFBs) could enable widespread integration of renewable energy, but only if costs are sufficiently low.

We review known or hypothesized molecular decomposition mechanisms for all five classes of aqueous redox-active organics and organometallics for which cycling lifetime results have been reported: quinones, viologens, aza-aromatics, iron coordination complexes, and nitroxide radicals.

Capacity fade is observed to be time-denominated rather than cycle-denominated, with a temporal rate that can depend on molecular concentrations and electrolyte state of charge through, e.g., bimolecular decomposition mechanisms.

We then review measurement methods for capacity fade rate and find that simple galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling is inadequate for assessing capacity fade when fade rates are low or extremely low and recommend refining methods to include potential holds for accurately assessing molecular lifeti...[truncated]
We consider separately symmetric cell cycling results, the interpretation of which is simplified by the absence of a different counter-electrolyte.

To find the solution of the optimization problem defined for energy management, a strategy is proposed based on the examination of a set of weighting factors to form the Pareto front while the problem associated with each of them is assessed in a mixed-integer linear programming framework.
The studies are implemented in different locations considering scenarios for electrical system limitation and the place of the storage units.

According to the results, applying the proposed multiobjective framework successfully addresses the enhancement of energy delivery and the decrease in power output fluctuations in the hybrid offshore parks across all scenarios of electrical system limitation and combinational storage locations.
Based on the results, in addition to the increase in delivered energy, a decrease in power variations by around 40 % up to over 80 % is observed in the studied cases.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: direct recycling
describing the current state and potential of direct recycling as an alternative to traditional pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical methods for lithium-ion battery recycling

Efficient Recycling Processes for Lithium-Ion Batteries Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are an indispensable power source for electric vehicles, portable electronics, and renewable energy storage systems due to their high energy density and long cycle life.
Full text excerpt:
Pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical processes are the most common recycling methods but pose considerable difficulties.
The energy-intensive pyrometallurgical recycling process results in the loss of critical materials such as lithium and suffers from substantial emissions and high costs.
Solvent extraction, a hydrometallurgical method, offers energy-efficient recovery for lithium, cobalt, and nickel but requires hazardous chemicals and careful waste management.
Direct recycling is an alternative to traditional methods as it preserves the cathode active material (CAM) structure for quicker and cheaper regeneration.

Hybrid pathways, combining hydrometallurgical and direct recycling methods, provide a cost-effective, scalable solution for LIB recycling, maximizing material recovery with minimal waste and environmental risk.
The success of recycling methods depends on factors such as battery chemistry, the scalability of recovery processes, and the cost-effectiveness of waste material recovery.
Though pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical processes have secured their position in LIB recycling, research is proceeding toward newer approaches, such as direct and hybrid methods.

In this review, we describe the current state of direct recycling as an alternative to traditional pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical methods for recuperating these critical materials, particularly lithium.

references: Lukas Wiszniewski et al. (2025) Integrating pyro- and biohydrometallurgy in a green closed-loop lithium-ion battery recycling approach doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.116811

evidence_type_annotations.claim: pyrometallurgical pre-treatment and biohydrometallurgy
Improved metal leaching efficiency from NMC black mass using a combined pyro- and biohydrometallurgical approach

Abstract: State-of-the-art stand-alone recycling routes for lithium-ion batteries (LIB), such as pyrometallurgy or hydrometallurgy, face significant challenges, including high energy consumption, loss of valuable elements like lithium (Li) and susceptibility to a waste stream with varying cathode ch...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

The present work investigates the comparison of recovery targets when processing synthesized black mass between a standalone biohydrometallurgical process and a combined method, including an upstream pyrometallurgical process.
In this approach, black mass undergoes carbothermic reduction in the InduMelt reactor, in which volatile elements like Li are vaporized and extracted via the gas stream, producing a Li-free alloy.
Thermodynamic equilibrium calculations using FactSage TM showed the possibility of partial oxidation when using the alloy in an open-loop approach.
Within a closed-loop approach, the alloy was pulverized for downstream biohydrometallurgy.
Bioleaching experiments using adapted enriched cultures with synthetic pre-and untreated NMC811 and LFP black mass were performed at pulp densities (w/v) of 1 % and 10 %.
The highest leaching efficiency of up to 100 % for Nickel (Ni), Manganese (Mn), Cobalt (Co), and Aluminium (Al) was achieved in the 1 % pre-treated experiment.

Increasing the pulp density to 10 % reduced the leaching efficiency of these metals to less than 25% which could be attributed to factors such as the alkaline nature of the black mass, microbial inhibition and passivation due to precipitation.
Pyrometallurgical pre-treatment improved metal leaching from NMC by up to 90 %, but had no impact on LFP.
